docs.title: krach
docs.text:
Live coding audio system. Define synths in Python, sequence them with composable patterns, hear them instantly.

import krach.dsp as krs

@kr.dsp
def acid_bass() -> krs.Signal:
    freq = krs.control("freq", 55.0, 20.0, 800.0)
    gate = krs.control("gate", 0.0, 0.0, 1.0)
    cutoff = krs.control("cutoff", 800.0, 100.0, 4000.0)
    env = krs.adsr(0.005, 0.15, 0.3, 0.08, gate)
    return krs.lowpass(krs.saw(freq), cutoff) * env * 0.55

kr.voice("bass", acid_bass, gain=0.3)
kr.play("bass", kr.seq("A2", "D3", None, "E2").over(2))

docs.title: What makes krach different
docs.text:
• Synths are Python functions — write DSP code, it compiles to FAUST and JIT-compiles to native audio via LLVM. Hot reload on save.
  • Patterns are composable — TidalCycles-inspired algebra. + sequences, | layers, .over() stretches, .swing() grooves. All operations return patterns — infinite composition.
  • One process, zero latency — Rust engine runs pattern sequencer + audio graph + FAUST JIT in a single binary. Python only sends pattern IR once — all per-cycle work is Rust.
  • Two symbols — kr is the mixer (voices, patterns, transport). krs is DSP primitives (oscillators, filters, envelopes). That's the entire API.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: krach is a live coding system for music. A Python REPL sends pattern and audio commands over a Unix socket to a single Rust binary that sequences events and renders audio in real time.

docs.title: Monorepo structure
docs.text:
noise/
├── audio-engine/       Rust lib  — graph-based audio engine (NodeFactory, DspGraph, GraphSwapper)
├── audio-faust/        Rust lib  — FAUST LLVM JIT plugin, hot reload via file watcher
├── pattern-engine/     Rust lib  — pattern sequencer (IR compiler, scheduler, MIDI/OSC output)
├── krach-engine/       Rust bin  — unified process (pattern-engine + audio-engine + audio-faust)
├── faust-dsl/          Python    — Python → Faust .dsp transpiler
└── krach/              Python    — live coding REPL (starts krach-engine, sends commands)

audio-engine is DSP-agnostic. It provides a graph of DspNode instances wired together, processed in topological order, with lock-free graph swaps and linear crossfades. No synthesis code lives here — just wiring, mixing, and control routing.

audio-faust compiles FAUST .dsp files via LLVM JIT into DspNode implementations and registers them with audio-engine's NodeRegistry. A file watcher triggers hot reload on save.

pattern-engine is a Tidal Cycles-inspired sequencer. It compiles a JSON IR (pattern trees) into arena-indexed CompiledPattern structs, evaluates them over rational time, and dispatches MIDI and OSC events on a real-time scheduler thread.

krach-engine links all three into one process. Pattern events that target the audio engine are dispatched directly via function calls — no network hop.

faust-dsl transpiles a Python DSL into FAUST .dsp source files.

krach is the user-facing Python REPL. It starts krach-engine as a subprocess, connects over a Unix socket, and exposes a concise API for live coding.

docs.title: Data flow
docs.text:
krach (Python REPL)
    │
    │  JSON over Unix socket (/tmp/krach.sock)
    │
    ▼
krach-engine (Rust, single process)
    │
    ├─── IPC thread ──── parse JSON ──── route by tag
    │         │                              │
    │    "cmd" tag                      "type" tag
    │         │                              │
    │         ▼                              ▼
    │    pattern-engine              audio-engine controller
    │    (EngineCommand)             (ClientMessage)
    │         │                              │
    │    scheduler thread            shadow graph + compiler
    │    (query patterns,            (compile, swap, crossfade)
    │     dispatch events)                   │
    │         │                         rtrb SPSC
    │         │                              │
    │         └──── direct fn call ──── AudioProcessor
    │                                        │
    │                                   DspGraph::process()
    │                                        │
    └─────────────────────────────────── cpal → CoreAudio

The IPC thread reads newline-delimited JSON from the socket. Messages with a "cmd" tag are pattern commands (SetPattern, Hush, etc.). Messages with a "type" tag are audio commands (load_graph, set_control, etc.). Both are sent over a single crossbeam_channel as LoopCommand variants to the main loop.

docs.title: Pattern compiler pipeline
docs.text:
Python IR (frozen dataclasses)
    │
    │  serialize to JSON
    ▼
IrNode (Rust, serde-tagged enum)
    │
    │  compile: validate, flatten into arena
    ▼
CompiledPattern (arena-indexed nodes)
    │
    │  query(arc): evaluate over rational time interval
    ▼
Vec<Event<Value>> (timed events with whole/part spans)
    │
    │  dispatch: match on Value type
    ▼
MIDI note-on/off  ──or──  audio-engine SetControl / SetAutomation

Pattern IR nodes: Atom, Silence, Cat, Stack, Fast, Slow, Early, Late, Rev, Every, Euclid, Degrade, Freeze.

Time is rational (i64/u64 numerator/denominator). Subdivisions are exact — no floating-point drift. Cat [a, b, c] splits a cycle into exact thirds. Fast [3, 2] compresses by 3/2.

The scheduler runs a real-time loop with 1ms sleep. It queries each active slot for the current cycle window plus a 100ms lookahead, collects events, and dispatches them via the appropriate output sink.

docs.title: NodeFactory and NodeRegistry
docs.text:
Every DSP type is registered as a (NodeTypeDecl, NodeFactory) pair. NodeTypeDecl declares ports (audio in/out) and controls (name, range, default). NodeFactory::create(sample_rate, block_size) returns a Box<dyn DspNode>.

Built-in types: Oscillator (sine/saw/square), DacNode. FAUST types are registered dynamically by audio-faust.

docs.title: DspGraph
docs.text: A topologically sorted DAG of DspNode instances. Processing walks nodes in order, passing scratch buffers between connected ports. No allocation during process().

docs.title: GraphSwapper
docs.text:
When the control thread compiles a new graph, it sends a SwapGraph command over an rtrb SPSC ring buffer. The audio thread crossfades linearly between old and new graphs over a configurable duration (default: half a beat at current BPM).

Node reuse: if the new graph contains a node with the same ID and type as the old graph, the existing instance is reused (preserving state, avoiding clicks).

docs.title: EngineController / AudioProcessor split
docs.text:
• EngineController (control thread): receives commands, maintains the shadow graph, compiles GraphIr → DspGraph, sends swap commands.
  • AudioProcessor (audio thread): drains commands from the SPSC ring, processes the active graph, writes to the output buffer. No locks, no allocation.

Connected by an rtrb single-producer single-consumer ring buffer.

docs.title: Automation
docs.text:
Automation drives node parameters through repeating (or one-shot) waveforms at block rate.

Available shapes: Sine, Tri, Ramp, RampDown, Square, Exp, Pulse { duty }, Custom { table }.

Each shape is evaluated at normalised time t in [0, 1) producing a value in [0, 1], then mapped to [lo, hi]. At 256-sample blocks and 44100 Hz sample rate, automation updates at ~172 Hz (once per block).

Automation is set via SetAutomation commands and cleared via ClearAutomation.

docs.title: FAUST JIT
docs.text:
~/.krach/dsp/
├── synth.dsp
├── filter.dsp
└── reverb.dsp
        │
        │  file watcher (notify crate)
        ▼
   FAUST LLVM JIT compiler
        │
        │  compile .dsp → machine code
        ▼
   DspNode implementation
        │
        │  register / re-register in NodeRegistry
        ▼
   Available as node type in audio graph

HotReloadEngine wraps an audio-engine with FAUST file watching. On startup, it scans a directory (default: ~/.krach/dsp/, override with NOISE_DSP_DIR) and registers all .dsp files. When a file is saved, it recompiles the FAUST code and calls reregister() on the registry, then reloads the current graph to pick up the new node.

FAUST LLVM JIT is not thread-safe — tests run serialized via .cargo/config.toml.

docs.title: Unified binary design
docs.text:
krach-engine is a single process with:

  • One socket (/tmp/krach.sock) for all IPC
  • One audio callback (cpal → CoreAudio)
  • One main loop that drains the command channel, queries patterns, dispatches events, and pumps the audio controller

Pattern events targeting the audio engine (OSC events with address /audio/set) are parsed and dispatched directly as SetControl calls — no UDP, no serialization.

MIDI note-offs are tracked in a BinaryHeap<PendingNoteOff> ordered by fire time. Audio control events use a similar pending queue for scheduled dispatch.

docs.title: Key design decisions
docs.text:
• Block-rate automation: parameter modulation happens once per audio block (~172 updates/sec at 256 samples / 44100 Hz), not per-sample. Good enough for musical LFOs, avoids per-sample branching.
  • Rational time: patterns use exact rational arithmetic. No floating-point accumulation means subdivisions stay perfectly aligned over arbitrarily long performances.
  • Lock-free audio thread: the audio callback never locks, never allocates, never blocks. All mutation happens on the control thread; the audio thread only reads.
  • Graph crossfade: swapping graphs crossfades linearly over half a beat. Old node instances are reused when IDs match, avoiding discontinuities.
  • Direct dispatch: in the unified binary, pattern → audio communication is a function call, not a network message. Latency is bounded by the scheduler's 1ms sleep + 100ms lookahead.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Env var Default Description NOISE_SOCKET /tmp/krach.sock IPC socket path NOISE_DSP_DIR ~/.krach/dsp/ FAUST .dsp file directory PATTERN_ENGINE_MIDI_CLOCK off Set to 1 for 24 ppqn MIDI clock

Engine defaults: 48 kHz sample rate, 256-sample blocks, stereo output.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Rust stable (edition 2024) via rustup
  • Python 3.13+
  • uv for Python dependency management
  • libfaust (FAUST LLVM JIT compiler) — brew install faust on macOS
  • socat (optional, for manual IPC testing)

docs.title: Dev setup
docs.text:
git clone <repo-url> noise
cd noise

# Build all Rust crates
cargo build --workspace

# Set up Python environments
cd krach && uv sync && cd ..
cd faust-dsl && uv sync && cd ..

docs.title: Rust (all crates)
docs.text:
cargo test --workspace

This runs tests for audio-engine, audio-faust, pattern-engine, and krach-engine. audio-faust tests run serialized (FAUST LLVM JIT is not thread-safe).

docs.title: Python — krach
docs.text:
cd krach
uv run pyright    # type checking (strict mode)
uv run pytest     # unit tests

docs.title: Python — faust-dsl
docs.text:
cd faust-dsl
uv run pytest

docs.title: Quick check before committing
docs.text:
cargo check --workspace && cargo test --workspace
cd krach && uv run pyright && uv run pytest && cd ..
cd faust-dsl && uv run pytest && cd ..

docs.title: Code style
docs.text:
The full style guide is in CODING_STYLE.md at the repo root. The short version:

Flat data, obvious flow, no unnecessary abstraction. Every type must map to a domain noun. Every abstraction must prevent a nameable bug.

docs.title: The abstraction test
docs.text:
Before adding any abstraction, ask:

  1. Can I name the specific bug this prevents? Not a category — a scenario in this code.
  2. Does the reader need to understand the abstraction to understand the data flow?
  3. Can I delete this and replace it with a function?

If you cannot answer (1) with a concrete scenario, do not add the abstraction.

docs.title: Rust guidelines
docs.text:
• Structs with public fields, free functions that transform them
  • Vec with with_capacity for hot paths — no heap allocation in audio callbacks
  • Templates/generics for callback inlining — no Box<dyn Fn> on hot paths
  • assert! / debug_assert! for contract violations
  • Result<T, E> for recoverable errors, fail loudly and early
  • Single-file modules up to ~500 lines — do not split prematurely
  • Short names: count, key, scratch — not eventCounter, temporaryScratchBuffer
  • No inheritance, no virtual dispatch, no trait objects unless runtime-polymorphic
  • No HashMap for n < 100 — use linear scan
  • No async unless actual I/O concurrency demands it
  • No design patterns by name (visitor, builder, factory, etc.)
  • Strict clippy lints, unsafe_code = "forbid"

docs.title: Python guidelines
docs.text:
• Strict pyright type checking — no Any escape hatches
  • Frozen dataclasses for IR types (immutable value objects)
  • Pattern matching (match/case) over if-elif chains
  • All deps managed through uv, never pip
  • Each subproject has its own venv and pyproject.toml

docs.title: Project structure
docs.text:
noise/
├── audio-engine/       Rust lib  — graph-based audio engine
├── audio-faust/        Rust lib  — FAUST LLVM JIT plugin + hot reload
├── pattern-engine/     Rust lib  — pattern sequencer
├── krach-engine/       Rust bin  — unified process
├── faust-dsl/          Python    — Python → Faust transpiler
└── krach/              Python    — live coding REPL

See the architecture documentation for detailed data flow and design decisions.

docs.title: Where things live
docs.text: Change Location New DSP node type audio-faust/ (FAUST .dsp) or audio-engine/src/nodes/ (Rust) Pattern combinator pattern-engine/src/ir.rs + pattern-engine/src/pattern.rs Python pattern API krach/src/krach/patterns/ IPC protocol krach-engine/src/ipc.rs + audio-engine/src/protocol.rs + pattern-engine/src/ipc/ FAUST transpiler faust-dsl/ REPL commands krach/src/krach/

docs.title: Adding a new pattern combinator
docs.text:
1. Add the variant to IrNode in pattern-engine/src/ir.rs
  2. Add compile logic in pattern-engine/src/pattern.rs
  3. Add the Python IR dataclass in krach/src/krach/patterns/ir.py
  4. Add serialization in krach/src/krach/patterns/serialize.py
  5. Write tests in both Rust and Python

docs.title: Adding a new audio node type
docs.text:
For FAUST nodes: write a .dsp file and drop it in ~/.krach/dsp/. The hot reload engine picks it up automatically.

For Rust nodes: implement DspNode and register via NodeTypeDecl + NodeFactory. See audio-engine/src/nodes/ for examples.

docs.title: Commit guidelines
docs.text:
• Small, focused commits — one logical change per commit
  • Clear commit messages describing why, not just what
  • Tests first: if you are fixing a bug, write a failing test before the fix
  • Run the full test suite before pushing

docs.title: PR process
docs.text:
1. Create a feature branch from main
  2. Make small commits with clear messages
  3. Ensure all tests pass (cargo test --workspace, uv run pytest in Python projects)
  4. Ensure type checks pass (cargo check --workspace, uv run pyright in krach)
  5. Open a PR with a description of the change and how to test it

docs.title: Effect Routing
docs.text: krach routes audio between nodes using the >> operator. Every audio node -- sources and effects alike -- is created with kr.node(). The system auto-detects whether a DSP is a source (0 audio inputs) or an effect (1+ audio inputs) from the DSP definition.

docs.title: The >> operator
docs.text:
>> is the primary routing method. It connects one node's output to another node's input:

bass = kr.node("bass", acid_bass, gain=0.3)
verb = kr.node("verb", reverb_fn, gain=0.3)

bass >> verb                  # route at unity gain
bass >> (verb, 0.4)           # route with send level (40%)

Chains work naturally:

mic = kr.input("mic")
filt = kr.node("filt", filter_fn, gain=1.0)
verb = kr.node("verb", reverb_fn, gain=0.3)

mic >> filt >> verb           # mic -> filter -> reverb

>> returns the target node, so chaining always reads left-to-right.

docs.title: kr.node() -- unified constructor
docs.text:
kr.node() replaces the old kr.voice() / kr.bus() split. It detects the node type from the DSP's num_inputs:

# Source (0 audio inputs) -- generates audio
bass = kr.node("bass", acid_bass, gain=0.3)

# Effect (1+ audio inputs) -- receives audio from sends/routes
verb = kr.node("verb", reverb_fn, gain=0.3)

No need to decide upfront. If the DSP has audio input controls, it becomes an effect node automatically.

Backward compatibility

kr.voice() and kr.bus() still work as aliases for kr.node(). Existing code does not need to change.

docs.title: kr.connect() -- explicit routing
docs.text:
The explicit API equivalent of >>:

kr.connect("bass", "verb", level=0.4)      # gain-controlled send
kr.connect("kick", "comp", port="in0")     # direct wire to port

Use kr.connect() when building routing from strings (e.g., in loops or abstractions). Use >> for interactive REPL work.

docs.title: Voice vs effect auto-detection
docs.text: Source (0 inputs) Effect (1+ inputs) Purpose Sound source (synth, sampler) Processor (reverb, delay, compressor) Created with kr.node() (auto) kr.node() (auto) Receives routes No Yes Old API kr.voice() kr.bus()

docs.title: Send levels
docs.text:
The dry signal from a source always goes to master. A send is a parallel copy scaled by the level:

bass >> (verb, 0.4)           # 40% to reverb, dry still goes to master

Update the send level at any time (no graph rebuild):

bass >> (verb, 0.7)           # change to 70%
# or explicitly:
kr.connect("bass", "verb", level=0.7)

docs.title: Direct wires (port assignment)
docs.text:
Wire a node directly to a specific input port. No gain stage -- the signal passes through as-is:

kr.connect("kick", "comp", port="in0")
kr.connect("snare", "comp", port="in1")

Use wires for multi-input effects like sidechain compressors or mixers where you need explicit port assignment.

docs.title: Reverb send
docs.text:
Multiple sources share one reverb:

@kr.dsp
def reverb_fx() -> krs.Signal:
    room = krs.control("room", 0.6, 0.0, 1.0)
    sig = krs.control("in0", 0.0, -1.0, 1.0)
    return krs.reverb(sig, room)

verb = kr.node("verb", reverb_fx, gain=0.3)

bass >> (verb, 0.4)
lead >> (verb, 0.6)
pad >> (verb, 0.5)

docs.title: Parallel compression
docs.text:
Wire drums to a compressor:

comp = kr.node("comp", compressor_fn, gain=0.5)
kr.connect("kick", "comp", port="in0")
kr.connect("snare", "comp", port="in1")

docs.title: Effect chain
docs.text:
Route through multiple effects in series:

filt = kr.node("filt", filter_fn, gain=1.0)
verb = kr.node("verb", reverb_fn, gain=0.3)

bass >> filt >> verb

docs.title: Multi-input mixer bus
docs.text:
Route several sources to a submix:

drums = kr.node("drums_bus", mixer_fn, gain=0.8)
kr.connect("kick", "drums_bus", port="in0")
kr.connect("snare", "drums_bus", port="in1")
kr.connect("hat", "drums_bus", port="in2")

docs.title: Control access with []
docs.text:
Set and read controls directly on node handles:

verb["room"] = 0.8
verb["room"]              # returns 0.8
bass["cutoff"] = 1200

Or use the explicit API:

kr.set("verb/room", 0.8)

docs.title: Group operations with / prefix
docs.text:
Node names with / act as groups. Operations on the prefix affect all matching nodes:

kr.node("drums/kick", kick_fn, gain=0.8)
kr.node("drums/hat", hat_fn, gain=0.6)
kr.node("drums/snare", snare_fn, gain=0.7)

# Adjust gain for all drums at once
kr.gain("drums", 0.4)

# Mute/solo the group
kr.mute("drums")
kr.solo("drums")

# Hush all drum patterns
kr.hush("drums")

docs.title: kr.gain() -- works on all nodes
docs.text:
Set gain without rebuilding the audio graph:

kr.gain("bass", 0.15)     # source gain
kr.gain("verb", 0.5)      # effect gain
kr.gain("drums", 0.4)     # group gain

docs.title: Smooth fades
docs.text:
Use kr.fade() for gradual gain changes:

kr.fade("bass/gain", target=0.0, bars=4)     # fade out over 4 bars
kr.fade("verb/gain", target=0.8, bars=8)     # fade in reverb
kr.fade("bass/cutoff", target=200.0, bars=4) # fade a control

docs.title: Removing nodes
docs.text:
Remove a node and clean up all its routes:

kr.remove("bass")
kr.remove_bus("verb")

docs.title: Full routing example
docs.text:
# Define synths
@kr.dsp
def kick() -> krs.Signal:
    gate = krs.control("gate", 0.0, 0.0, 1.0)
    env = krs.adsr(0.001, 0.25, 0.0, 0.05, gate)
    return krs.sine_osc(55.0 + env * 200.0) * env * 0.9

@kr.dsp
def acid_bass() -> krs.Signal:
    freq = krs.control("freq", 55.0, 20.0, 800.0)
    gate = krs.control("gate", 0.0, 0.0, 1.0)
    cutoff = krs.control("cutoff", 800.0, 100.0, 4000.0)
    env = krs.adsr(0.005, 0.15, 0.3, 0.08, gate)
    return krs.lowpass(krs.saw(freq), cutoff) * env * 0.55

@kr.dsp
def reverb_fx() -> krs.Signal:
    room = krs.control("room", 0.6, 0.0, 1.0)
    sig = krs.control("in0", 0.0, -1.0, 1.0)
    return krs.reverb(sig, room)

# Set up nodes
with kr.batch():
    k = kr.node("kick", kick, gain=0.8)
    bass = kr.node("bass", acid_bass, gain=0.3)

verb = kr.node("verb", reverb_fx, gain=0.3)
bass >> (verb, 0.4)

# Play
kr.tempo = 128
k @ kr.hit() * 4
bass @ kr.seq("A2", "D3", None, "E2").over(2)
bass["cutoff"] = 1200

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• macOS (CoreAudio — Linux/Windows support planned)
  • Rust stable toolchain (rustup.rs)
  • Python 3.13+
  • uv — Python package manager
  • FAUST compiler + LLVM development headers 
    brew install faust llvm
export LLVM_SYS_181_PREFIX=$(brew --prefix llvm)

docs.title: Install
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/krachio/noise.git
cd noise

# Build the Rust engine (first build compiles FAUST bindings — takes ~2 min)
cargo build --release -p krach-engine

# Install the Python package
cd krach && uv sync && cd ..

docs.title: Start the REPL
docs.text:
./bin/krach

This starts the Rust audio engine in the background and opens an IPython REPL with two objects:

  • kr — the audio graph (nodes, routing, patterns, transport)
  • krs — DSP primitives (oscillators, filters, envelopes)

Engine logs go to ~/.krach/engine.log — tail them in a second terminal for debugging:

tail -f ~/.krach/engine.log

docs.title: 1. Define a kick drum
docs.text:
@kr.dsp
def kick() -> krs.Signal:
    gate = krs.control("gate", 0.0, 0.0, 1.0)
    env = krs.adsr(0.001, 0.25, 0.0, 0.05, gate)
    return krs.sine_osc(55.0 + env * 200.0) * env * 0.9

The @kr.dsp decorator transpiles your Python function to FAUST, compiles it via LLVM, and registers it as an audio node. The krs.control() calls define parameters that patterns can drive.

docs.title: 2. Create a voice and play it
docs.text:
kr.voice("kick", kick, gain=0.8)
kr.play("kick", kr.hit() * 4)

You should hear a four-on-the-floor kick. kr.hit() triggers the gate parameter. * 4 repeats it four times per cycle.

docs.title: 3. Change the tempo
docs.text:
kr.tempo = 140

The kick immediately speeds up. No restart, no rebuild.

docs.title: 4. Add a bass synth
docs.text:
@kr.dsp
def bass() -> krs.Signal:
    freq = krs.control("freq", 55.0, 20.0, 800.0)
    gate = krs.control("gate", 0.0, 0.0, 1.0)
    cutoff = krs.control("cutoff", 800.0, 100.0, 4000.0)
    env = krs.adsr(0.005, 0.15, 0.3, 0.08, gate)
    return krs.lowpass(krs.saw(freq), cutoff) * env * 0.55

bass = kr.voice("bass", bass, gain=0.3)

docs.title: 5. Play a bass line
docs.text:
bass @ kr.seq("A2", "D3", None, "E2").over(2)

The @ operator plays a pattern on a node. You can also use kr.play("bass", pattern) for the explicit form.

kr.seq() creates a sequence of notes. None is a rest. .over(2) stretches the pattern to 2 cycles (8 beats at meter=4).

docs.title: 6. Modulate the filter
docs.text:
bass @ ("cutoff", kr.sine(200, 2000).over(4))

The @ operator also accepts (param, pattern) tuples for control modulation. The cutoff sweeps between 200 Hz and 2000 Hz over 4 cycles with a sine wave.

docs.title: 7. Create a reverb and route the bass
docs.text:
@kr.dsp
def verb() -> krs.Signal:
    sig = krs.control("in", 0.0, -1.0, 1.0)
    room = krs.control("room", 0.6, 0.0, 1.0)
    return krs.reverb(sig, room)

reverb = kr.node("verb", verb, gain=0.3)

Note

kr.node() auto-detects whether a DSP has audio inputs. Effects (like reverb) are detected automatically -- no need to choose between voice() and bus().

docs.title: 8. Route the bass to reverb with >>
docs.text:
bass >> (reverb, 0.4)    # send at 40% level

The >> operator routes signal between nodes. Use a tuple (target, level) for gain-controlled sends, or just bass >> reverb for unity gain.

docs.title: Live performance
docs.text:
kr.mute("kick")          # silence the kick
kr.unmute("kick")         # bring it back
kr.fade("bass/gain", 0.0, bars=4)  # fade bass out over 4 bars
kr.solo("bass")           # mute everything except bass
kr.unsolo()               # unmute all
kr.hush("kick")           # stop kick pattern (voice stays, can replay)
kr.stop()                 # stop all patterns

docs.title: Saving your work
docs.text:
# Save to a reloadable Python file
kr.export("my_session.py")

# Later, in a new REPL session:
kr.load("my_session.py")

docs.title: Operator DSL cheat sheet
docs.text:
Operator Example Meaning >> bass >> verb Route signal >> bass >> (verb, 0.4) Route with send level @ bass @ pattern Play pattern @ bass @ "A2 D3 ~ E2" Play mini-notation @ bass @ ("cutoff", pat) Modulate control @ bass @ None Hush [] bass["cutoff"] = 1200 Set control [] bass["cutoff"] Get control value

All operators have explicit equivalents: kr.connect(), kr.play(), kr.set().

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Synth Design — deep dive into @kr.dsp and krs primitives
  • Patterns — pattern algebra, combinators, composition
  • Effect Routing — node routing, >> operator, sends, wires

docs.title: MIDI and Hardware
docs.text: krach connects to external MIDI controllers and audio hardware for hands-on control of your live coding session.

docs.title: MIDI CC mapping
docs.text:
Map a MIDI continuous controller to any voice or bus parameter:

kr.midi_map(cc=74, path="bass/cutoff", lo=200.0, hi=4000.0)

The CC value (0--127) is scaled linearly to the lo--hi range and applied to the parameter in real time.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description cc MIDI CC number (0--127) path Control path, e.g. "bass/cutoff", "verb/room" lo Value when CC is 0 hi Value when CC is 127 channel MIDI channel (optional, defaults to all channels)

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Knob 74 controls bass filter cutoff
kr.midi_map(cc=74, path="bass/cutoff", lo=200.0, hi=4000.0)

# Knob 1 (mod wheel) controls bass gain, channel 5 only
kr.midi_map(cc=1, path="bass/gain", lo=0.0, hi=1.0, channel=5)

# Map reverb room size to a fader
kr.midi_map(cc=7, path="verb/room", lo=0.0, hi=1.0)

# Master volume on a fader
kr.midi_map(cc=7, path="master", lo=0.0, hi=1.0, channel=1)

docs.title: Typical controller mappings
docs.text:
A practical starting point for an 8-knob MIDI controller:

kr.midi_map(cc=70, path="kick/gain", lo=0.0, hi=1.0)
kr.midi_map(cc=71, path="hat/gain", lo=0.0, hi=1.0)
kr.midi_map(cc=72, path="bass/gain", lo=0.0, hi=1.0)
kr.midi_map(cc=73, path="lead/gain", lo=0.0, hi=1.0)
kr.midi_map(cc=74, path="bass/cutoff", lo=200.0, hi=4000.0)
kr.midi_map(cc=75, path="lead/cutoff", lo=500.0, hi=8000.0)
kr.midi_map(cc=76, path="verb/room", lo=0.0, hi=1.0)
kr.midi_map(cc=77, path="verb/gain", lo=0.0, hi=0.8)

docs.title: ADC input: live audio
docs.text:
Capture audio from your system's audio interface (CoreAudio on macOS):

mic = kr.input("mic", channel=0, gain=0.5)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description name Label for the input (e.g. "mic", "guitar") channel ADC input channel (0-indexed) gain Input gain (0.0--1.0)

docs.title: Routing input to effects
docs.text:
Live audio inputs can be sent to buses just like voices:

mic = kr.input("mic", channel=0, gain=0.5)
verb = kr.bus("verb", reverb_fn, gain=0.3)

mic.send(verb, 0.4)   # send mic to reverb at 40%

docs.title: Multiple inputs
docs.text:
mic = kr.input("mic", channel=0, gain=0.5)
guitar = kr.input("guitar", channel=1, gain=0.7)

mic.send(verb, 0.3)
guitar.send(verb, 0.5)

docs.title: MIDI clock output
docs.text:
krach can send MIDI clock to sync external hardware (drum machines, sequencers, synths). Enable it with an environment variable before starting the REPL:

KRACH_MIDI_CLOCK=1 ./bin/krach

The clock follows kr.tempo and sends standard MIDI clock messages (24 ppqn) to all connected MIDI outputs.

docs.title: Hardware setup tips
docs.text:
Audio interface -- krach uses CoreAudio on macOS. Set your preferred interface in Audio MIDI Setup before starting the REPL. Lower buffer sizes give lower latency but higher CPU load.

MIDI controllers -- any class-compliant USB MIDI controller works. No driver installation needed on macOS. Plug in before starting the REPL.

Channel isolation -- use the channel= parameter on kr.midi_map() when multiple controllers are connected, to avoid CC collisions.

Persisting mappings -- save your MIDI mappings in a setup script and load it at the start of each session:

# midi_setup.py
kr.midi_map(cc=74, path="bass/cutoff", lo=200.0, hi=4000.0)
kr.midi_map(cc=1, path="bass/gain", lo=0.0, hi=1.0)
kr.midi_map(cc=7, path="verb/room", lo=0.0, hi=1.0)

# In the REPL
kr.load("midi_setup.py")

Monitoring latency -- for live performance with external audio input, keep your audio buffer size at 128 or 256 samples. At 44.1 kHz, 128 samples is about 3 ms of latency.

docs.title: Mini-Notation
docs.text: kr.p() parses a compact string notation into a pattern. It is a shorthand for building patterns without chaining Python calls.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
kr.p("x . x . x . . x")

Returns a Pattern object -- composable like any other pattern.

docs.title: Tokens
docs.text: Token Meaning Equivalent x or X Hit (percussive trigger) kr.hit() . Rest kr.rest() ~ Rest (alternative) kr.rest() - Rest (alternative) kr.rest() C4, Cs4, C#4 Note by pitch name kr.note("C4")

docs.title: Grouping: []
docs.text:
Square brackets play tokens simultaneously (stacked):

kr.p("[C4 E4] G4 B4")    # C4 and E4 together, then G4, then B4
kr.p("[x x] . x .")      # double hit, rest, hit, rest

docs.title: Repeat: *N
docs.text:
Repeat a token N times:

kr.p("C4*2 E4 G4")       # C4 C4 E4 G4 (C4 played twice)
kr.p("x*4")              # equivalent to kr.hit() * 4

docs.title: Drum patterns
docs.text:
# Basic 4/4 kick
kr.play("kick", kr.p("x . . . x . . . x . . . x . . ."))

# Shorter: 4 hits with rests between
kr.play("kick", kr.p("x . x . x . . x"))

# Hi-hat 8ths
kr.play("hat", kr.p("x x x x x x x x"))

docs.title: Melodic patterns
docs.text:
# Simple melody
kr.play("lead", kr.p("C4 E4 G4 ~ C5"))

# With simultaneous notes
kr.play("pad", kr.p("[C4 E4] [D4 F4] [E4 G4] ~"))

docs.title: Composable
docs.text:
Mini-notation patterns support all the same transforms:

kr.play("kick", kr.p("x . x . x . . x").swing(0.67))
kr.play("hat", kr.p("x x x x").over(2))
kr.play("bass", kr.p("C3 E3 G3 ~").every(4, lambda p: p.reverse()))

docs.title: Patterns
docs.text: Patterns are the sequencing system in krach. They describe what happens and when, using composable IR trees with rational time. Patterns are independent of voices -- you build a pattern, then bind it to a voice with kr.play().

docs.title: What is a pattern?
docs.text:
A pattern is a tree of events distributed over a cycle (one bar by default). The tree is pure data -- an intermediate representation that the Rust engine compiles to block-rate automation curves. No per-event IPC happens during playback.

Patterns are:

  • Composable -- combine with +, |, *, and transforms
  • Rational time -- subdivisions are exact, no floating-point drift
  • Reusable -- the same pattern can play on different voices

docs.title: Atoms
docs.text: Atoms are the smallest pattern elements. All pattern builders live on the kr namespace.

docs.title: kr.note() -- melodic trigger
docs.text:
Sets freq and fires gate (trigger + reset). Accepts multiple pitch formats:

kr.note(440.0)                          # float Hz
kr.note("C4")                           # string pitch name
kr.note(60)                             # int MIDI note number (converted via mtof)
kr.note(440.0, vel=0.7, cutoff=1200.0)  # extra params set alongside freq/gate

docs.title: Note syntax details
docs.text:
Float -- interpreted as Hz directly:

kr.note(440.0)   # A4
kr.note(55.0)    # A1

String -- pitch name with octave. Sharps use s or #:

kr.note("C4")    # middle C
kr.note("Cs4")   # C sharp 4
kr.note("C#4")   # also C sharp 4
kr.note("Bb3")   # B flat 3

Integer -- MIDI note number, converted to Hz internally:

kr.note(60)      # middle C (261.63 Hz)
kr.note(69)      # A4 (440 Hz)

docs.title: Chords (multiple simultaneous pitches)
docs.text:
Pass multiple pitches to kr.note() for a chord:

kr.note("A4", "C5", "E5")           # A minor triad
kr.note(220.0, 330.0, 440.0)        # same as Hz

Or use the | (layer) operator:

kr.note("A4") | kr.note("C5") | kr.note("E5")

Chords require polyphony

The voice must have count >= the number of simultaneous pitches:

kr.voice("rhodes", rhodes_fn, gain=0.3, count=4)  # poly voice
kr.play("rhodes", kr.note("A4", "C5", "E5") + kr.rest())

docs.title: kr.hit() -- percussive trigger
docs.text:
Fires a gate trigger without setting pitch. Use for drums and one-shot sounds:

kr.hit()           # triggers "gate" control
kr.hit("kick")     # triggers a custom-named control

docs.title: kr.rest() -- silence
docs.text:
A single beat of silence:

kr.rest()

docs.title: kr.seq() -- sequential notes
docs.text:
Plays notes one at a time, in sequence. Use None for rests:

kr.seq(55.0, 73.0, None, 65.0)     # Hz values, with a rest
kr.seq("C4", "E4", "G4")           # pitch names
kr.seq("A2", "D3", None, "E2")     # bass line with rest

kr.seq() also accepts kr.note() objects, letting you set per-note params:

kr.seq(
    kr.note(220.0, cutoff=800.0),
    kr.note(330.0, cutoff=1200.0),
    None,
    kr.note(440.0)
)

# Mix pitch strings and note objects freely:
kr.seq("A2", "D3", kr.note("E2", vel=0.5), None)

kr.seq() is NOT for chords

kr.seq("A4", "C5", "E5") plays three notes one after another -- it is a melody, not a chord. For chords, use kr.note("A4", "C5", "E5").

docs.title: + -- sequence
docs.text:
Concatenates patterns in time, dividing the cycle equally:

kr.note("C4") + kr.note("E4") + kr.note("G4")  # 3 notes, each 1/3 cycle

docs.title: | -- layer (simultaneous)
docs.text:
Stacks patterns to play at the same time:

kr.note("A4") | kr.note("C5") | kr.note("E5")  # chord

docs.title: * -- repeat
docs.text:
Repeats a pattern N times within one cycle:

kr.hit() * 4           # 4-on-the-floor
kr.hit() * 8           # 8th notes
(kr.hit() + kr.rest()) * 4  # offbeat pattern, repeated 4x

docs.title: .over(n) -- stretch to N cycles
docs.text:
Stretches a pattern to span multiple bars:

kr.seq("A2", "D3", None, "E2").over(2)   # 4 notes over 2 bars
kr.ramp(200.0, 2000.0).over(4)           # 4-bar ramp

docs.title: .fast(n) -- speed up
docs.text:
Doubles (or triples, etc.) the playback speed:

p = kr.hit() * 4
kr.play("kick", p.fast(2))   # 8 hits per cycle

docs.title: .slow(n) -- slow down
docs.text:
The inverse of .fast():

p.slow(2)   # half speed, pattern spans 2 cycles

docs.title: .every(n, fn) -- periodic transform
docs.text:
Apply a transform every Nth cycle:

p = kr.hit() * 4
p.every(4, lambda p: p.reverse())    # reverse every 4th bar
p.every(3, lambda p: p.fast(2))      # double time every 3rd bar

docs.title: .reverse() -- reverse
docs.text:
Plays the pattern backwards:

kr.seq("A2", "D3", "E3", "G3").reverse()

docs.title: .spread(hits, steps) -- euclidean rhythm
docs.text:
Distributes hits evenly across steps slots:

kr.hit().spread(3, 8)    # 3 hits in 8 steps (tresillo)
kr.hit().spread(5, 8)    # 5 hits in 8 steps (cinquillo)
kr.hit().spread(7, 16)   # 7 hits in 16 steps

docs.title: .thin(probability) -- degrade
docs.text:
Randomly drops events with the given probability:

kr.hit() * 8
(kr.hit() * 8).thin(0.3)   # randomly drop 30% of hits

docs.title: .mask(mask_str) -- selective silence
docs.text:
Suppress events at specific positions using a mask string. 1/x = keep, 0/./~ = silence:

kr.seq("A2", "D3", "E2").mask("1 1 0")   # silences the third event
kr.seq("C4", "E4", "G4", "B4").mask("x . x .")  # play 1st and 3rd only

docs.title: .sometimes(prob, fn) -- probabilistic transform
docs.text:
Apply a transform with probability prob each cycle. On cycles where it does not fire, the original pattern plays:

p = kr.hit() * 4
p.sometimes(0.3, lambda p: p.reverse())   # reverse 30% of cycles
p.sometimes(0.5, lambda p: p.fast(2))     # double time 50% of the time

docs.title: kr.cat() -- cycle-level concatenation
docs.text:
Plays each pattern for one full cycle, then loops:

a = kr.seq("A2", "D3", None, "E2")
b = kr.seq("C3", "E3", "G3", "B3")
kr.play("bass", kr.cat(a, b))  # a for 1 cycle, b for 1 cycle, repeat

This is equivalent to (a + b).over(2) but reads more clearly for multi-cycle structures.

docs.title: kr.stack() -- layer patterns
docs.text:
Plays all patterns simultaneously. Same as the | operator, but accepts any number of arguments:

kr.stack(
    kr.note("A4"),
    kr.note("C5"),
    kr.note("E5"),
)
# equivalent to: kr.note("A4") | kr.note("C5") | kr.note("E5")

docs.title: kr.struct() -- impose rhythm onto melody
docs.text:
Takes the onset positions from a rhythm pattern and fills them with values from a melody pattern (cycling if melody is shorter):

rhythm = kr.p("x . x x . x . .")
melody = kr.seq("A2", "D3", "E2")
kr.play("bass", kr.struct(rhythm, melody))
# plays A2, D3, E2, A2 at the rhythm's hit positions

docs.title: Continuous patterns
docs.text: Continuous patterns generate smooth control sweeps. Use them for modulation, filter control, and automation. All return Pattern objects and compose with .over(), +, etc.

docs.title: kr.sine(lo, hi) -- sine sweep
docs.text:
kr.sine(200, 2000)             # one-cycle sine from 200..2000
kr.sine(200, 2000).over(4)     # 4-cycle sine sweep

docs.title: kr.saw(lo, hi) -- sawtooth ramp
docs.text:
kr.saw(200, 2000)              # ramp up from 200 to 2000
kr.saw(200, 2000).over(8)      # 8-cycle ramp

docs.title: kr.rand(lo, hi) -- random values
docs.text:
kr.rand(200, 2000)             # random values between 200..2000

Different values each cycle. Use .over(N) to slow the change rate.

docs.title: Using continuous patterns
docs.text:
Play them on a control path or use the @ operator:

# With @ operator
bass @ ("cutoff", kr.sine(200, 2000).over(4))

# With kr.play()
kr.play("bass/cutoff", kr.sine(200, 2000).over(4))

docs.title: Modulation patterns
docs.text:
Modulation patterns generate continuous control values instead of note triggers. All return Pattern objects and compose like any other pattern.

kr.mod_sine(lo, hi)         # sine LFO between lo and hi
kr.mod_tri(lo, hi)          # triangle LFO
kr.mod_ramp(lo, hi)         # ramp up (sawtooth)
kr.mod_ramp_down(lo, hi)    # ramp down
kr.mod_square(lo, hi)       # square LFO
kr.mod_exp(lo, hi)          # exponential curve
kr.ramp(start, end)         # one-shot linear ramp

All accept an optional steps=64 parameter to control resolution.

docs.title: Using modulation patterns
docs.text:
Play them on a control path:

# Sine LFO on bass cutoff over 4 bars
kr.play("bass/cutoff", kr.mod_sine(200.0, 2000.0).over(4))

# Triangle LFO on gain
kr.play("bass/gain", kr.mod_tri(0.1, 0.5).over(8))

# One-shot ramp
kr.play("bass/cutoff", kr.ramp(200.0, 2000.0).over(4))

Or use the kr.mod() shorthand:

kr.mod("bass/cutoff", kr.mod_sine(200.0, 2000.0), bars=4)
kr.mod("bass/gain", kr.mod_tri(0.1, 0.5), bars=8)

Stop a modulation with kr.hush():

kr.hush("bass/cutoff")

docs.title: @ operator (REPL-friendly)
docs.text:
The @ operator on a node handle plays a pattern immediately:

bass @ kr.seq("A2", "D3", None, "E2").over(2)    # play pattern
bass @ "A2 D3 ~ E2"                               # play mini-notation string
bass @ ("cutoff", kr.sine(200, 2000).over(4))      # modulate a control
bass @ None                                         # hush

docs.title: kr.play(target, pattern) (explicit)
docs.text:
Binds a pattern to a node or control path. The pattern starts on the next cycle boundary:

kr.play("kick", kr.hit() * 4)
kr.play("bass", kr.seq("A2", "D3", None, "E2").over(2))
kr.play("bass/cutoff", kr.ramp(200.0, 2000.0).over(4))

docs.title: Node handles
docs.text:
kr.node(), kr.voice(), and kr.bus() all return a handle:

bass = kr.node("bass", acid_bass, gain=0.3)

bass @ kr.seq("A2", "D3", None, "E2").over(2)
bass["cutoff"] = 1200
bass.fade("cutoff", 200, bars=4)
bass @ ("cutoff", kr.sine(400, 2000).over(4))
bass.mute()

docs.title: Convenience kwargs
docs.text:
kr.play() accepts swing= as a keyword argument:

kr.play("kick", kr.hit() * 8, swing=0.67)

docs.title: Pattern retrieval
docs.text:
Get the current pattern from a voice to modify and replay:

p = kr.pattern("kick")            # get current pattern by name
kr.play("kick", p.fast(2))       # modify and replay

# Or via a voice handle:
kick = kr.voice("drums/kick", kick_fn, gain=0.8)
p = kick.pattern()
kick.play(p.every(4, lambda p: p.reverse()))

docs.title: How binding works
docs.text:
Pattern atoms like kr.note() and kr.hit() produce bare parameter names (e.g., freq, gate). When you call kr.play("bass", pattern), the system binds those bare names to the voice's control namespace:

  • freq becomes bass/freq
  • gate becomes bass/gate
  • cutoff becomes bass/cutoff

This means the same pattern can be reused on different voices:

melody = kr.seq("A2", "D3", None, "E2").over(2)
kr.play("bass", melody)
kr.play("lead", melody)   # same pattern, different voice

docs.title: 4-on-the-floor kick
docs.text: kr.play("kick", kr.hit() * 4)

docs.title: Offbeat hi-hat
docs.text: kr.play("hat", (kr.rest() + kr.hit()) * 4)

docs.title: Bass line with rests
docs.text: kr.play("bass", kr.seq("A2", "C3", "D3", "E3").over(2))

docs.title: Chord stabs
docs.text:
kr.voice("rhodes", rhodes_fn, gain=0.3, count=4)
kr.play("rhodes", kr.note("A4", "C5", "E5") + kr.rest())

docs.title: Euclidean hi-hat with degradation
docs.text: kr.play("hat", kr.hit().spread(5, 8).thin(0.2))

docs.title: Evolving pattern
docs.text:
p = kr.hit() * 4
kr.play("kick", p.every(4, lambda p: p.fast(2)))

docs.title: Protocol Reference
docs.text: krach uses a single Unix socket for all communication between the Python REPL and the Rust engine. Messages are newline-delimited JSON.

docs.title: Transport
docs.text:
• Socket: /tmp/krach.sock (override with NOISE_SOCKET env var)
  • Format: one JSON object per line, terminated by \n
  • Direction: request/response — the client sends a command, the server replies with one JSON line

docs.title: Message families
docs.text:
The IPC server accepts two distinct message families on the same socket:

Family Tag field Purpose Pattern commands "cmd" Control the pattern sequencer (set patterns, hush, tempo) Audio commands "type" Control the audio engine (load graphs, set controls, automation)

The IPC thread inspects the top-level tag to route each message to the correct subsystem.

docs.title: Pattern commands
docs.text: All pattern commands use a "cmd" tag.

docs.title: SetPattern
docs.text:
Assign a pattern to a named slot. The pattern starts playing from the current cycle position.

{"cmd": "SetPattern", "slot": "d1", "pattern": {"op": "Atom", "value": {"type": "Note", "channel": 0, "note": 60, "velocity": 100, "dur": 0.5}}}

docs.title: SetPatternFromZero
docs.text:
Same as SetPattern, but restarts the slot from cycle zero.

{"cmd": "SetPatternFromZero", "slot": "d1", "pattern": {"op": "Cat", "children": [...]}}

docs.title: Hush
docs.text:
Silence a single slot.

{"cmd": "Hush", "slot": "d1"}

docs.title: HushAll
docs.text:
Silence all slots.

{"cmd": "HushAll"}

docs.title: SetBpm
docs.text:
Change tempo. Takes effect on the next scheduler tick.

{"cmd": "SetBpm", "bpm": 140.0}

docs.title: SetBeatsPerCycle
docs.text:
Set meter (beats per cycle). Default is 4.

{"cmd": "SetBeatsPerCycle", "beats": 3}

docs.title: Batch
docs.text:
Atomic group of commands. All commands are validated before any are applied. If any command fails, the entire batch is rejected. Nested batches are not allowed.

{"cmd": "Batch", "commands": [
  {"cmd": "SetPattern", "slot": "d1", "pattern": {"op": "Atom", "value": {"type": "Note", "channel": 0, "note": 60, "velocity": 100, "dur": 0.5}}},
  {"cmd": "SetPattern", "slot": "d2", "pattern": {"op": "Silence"}},
  {"cmd": "SetBpm", "bpm": 140.0}
]}

docs.title: Ping
docs.text:
Health check.

{"cmd": "Ping"}

docs.title: Audio commands
docs.text: All audio commands use a "type" tag with snake_case variant names.

docs.title: load_graph
docs.text:
Replace the entire audio graph. Crossfades from the current graph. Nodes with matching IDs are reused.

{"type": "load_graph", "nodes": [
  {"id": "osc1", "type_id": "oscillator", "controls": {"freq": 440.0}},
  {"id": "out", "type_id": "dac", "controls": {}}
], "connections": [
  {"from_node": "osc1", "from_port": "out", "to_node": "out", "to_port": "in"}
], "exposed_controls": {"pitch": ["osc1", "freq"]}}

docs.title: set_control
docs.text:
Set an exposed control parameter by label.

{"type": "set_control", "label": "pitch", "value": 440.0}

docs.title: set_master_gain
docs.text:
Set the master output gain (0.0-1.0).

{"type": "set_master_gain", "gain": 0.5}

docs.title: set_automation
docs.text:
Add or replace a parameter automation. The shape drives the parameter between lo and hi with the given period.

{"type": "set_automation", "id": "lfo1", "label": "pitch", "shape": "sine", "lo": 220.0, "hi": 880.0, "period_secs": 2.0, "one_shot": false}

Available shapes: sine, tri, ramp, ramp_down, square, exp, pulse.

docs.title: clear_automation
docs.text:
Remove an automation by ID.

{"type": "clear_automation", "id": "lfo1"}

docs.title: add_node / remove_node / connect / disconnect
docs.text:
Incremental graph mutations. These modify the shadow graph and trigger a recompile.

{"type": "add_node", "id": "filt1", "type_id": "lpf", "controls": {"cutoff": 1000.0}}
{"type": "remove_node", "id": "filt1"}
{"type": "connect", "from_node": "osc1", "from_port": "out", "to_node": "filt1", "to_port": "in"}
{"type": "disconnect", "from_node": "osc1", "from_port": "out", "to_node": "filt1", "to_port": "in"}

docs.title: graph_batch
docs.text:
Atomic batch of graph mutations. All mutations are applied to the shadow graph before a single recompile.

{"type": "graph_batch", "commands": [
  {"type": "add_node", "id": "filt1", "type_id": "lpf", "controls": {"cutoff": 1000.0}},
  {"type": "connect", "from_node": "osc1", "from_port": "out", "to_node": "filt1", "to_port": "in"}
]}

docs.title: list_nodes
docs.text:
Request the list of registered node type IDs. The engine sends a response with all available types.

{"type": "list_nodes", "reply_port": 9001}

docs.title: ping
docs.text:
Health check for the audio engine.

{"type": "ping"}

docs.title: shutdown
docs.text:
Stop the engine.

{"type": "shutdown"}

docs.title: Pattern IR format
docs.text: Patterns are JSON trees tagged by "op". Every pattern occupies one cycle (one bar). Children of Cat divide the cycle equally; children of Stack overlap the full cycle.

docs.title: Combinators
docs.text: Op Fields Description Atom value Single event, plays once per cycle Silence — No events Cat children Sequential: children share the cycle equally Stack children Parallel: all children occupy the full cycle Fast factor, child Speed up by rational factor [num, den] Slow factor, child Slow down by rational factor [num, den] Early offset, child Shift events earlier by rational offset Late offset, child Shift events later by rational offset Rev child Reverse within each cycle Every n, transform, child Apply transform every n cycles Euclid pulses, steps, rotation, child Euclidean rhythm distribution Degrade prob, seed, child Random dropout with given probability Freeze child Mark sub-pattern as indivisible unit

docs.title: Value types
docs.text: Type Fields Description Note channel, note, velocity, dur MIDI note (note-off auto-scheduled) Cc channel, controller, value MIDI CC Osc address, args OSC message with typed args (Float, Int, Str) Control label, value Direct audio-engine control (no OSC serialization)

docs.title: Rational time
docs.text: Fast, Slow, Early, Late take rational values as [numerator, denominator]. Examples: [1, 1] = 1, [3, 2] = 1.5, [1, 3] = one third.

docs.title: Pattern command responses
docs.text:
{"status": "Ok", "msg": "pattern set on d1"}
{"status": "Error", "msg": "Cat requires at least one child"}
{"status": "Pong"}

docs.title: Audio command responses
docs.text:
{"type": "ok"}
{"type": "error", "message": "unknown node type: foo"}
{"type": "pong"}
{"type": "node_types", "types": ["oscillator", "dac", "synth", "lpf"]}

docs.title: Example with socat
docs.text:
# Ping the engine
echo '{"cmd": "Ping"}' | socat - UNIX-CONNECT:/tmp/krach.sock

# Set a pattern
echo '{"cmd": "SetPattern", "slot": "d1", "pattern": {"op": "Cat", "children": [{"op": "Atom", "value": {"type": "Note", "channel": 0, "note": 60, "velocity": 100, "dur": 0.5}}, {"op": "Atom", "value": {"type": "Note", "channel": 0, "note": 64, "velocity": 100, "dur": 0.5}}]}}' | socat - UNIX-CONNECT:/tmp/krach.sock

# Load an audio graph
echo '{"type": "load_graph", "nodes": [{"id": "osc1", "type_id": "oscillator", "controls": {"freq": 440.0}}, {"id": "out", "type_id": "dac", "controls": {}}], "connections": [{"from_node": "osc1", "from_port": "out", "to_node": "out", "to_port": "in"}], "exposed_controls": {"pitch": ["osc1", "freq"]}}' | socat - UNIX-CONNECT:/tmp/krach.sock

# Set a control
echo '{"type": "set_control", "label": "pitch", "value": 880.0}' | socat - UNIX-CONNECT:/tmp/krach.sock

# Hush all
echo '{"cmd": "HushAll"}' | socat - UNIX-CONNECT:/tmp/krach.sock

docs.title: Scenes
docs.text: krach can save and restore complete session states -- voices, buses, patterns, tempo, and all control values. Use scenes to build song sections, switch between arrangements, and persist your work to disk.

docs.title: In-memory snapshots
docs.text:
Save the current state to memory with a name:

kr.save("verse")

Restore it later:

kr.recall("verse")

In-memory snapshots are fast but lost when you exit the REPL.

docs.title: Workflow: A/B between sections
docs.text:
# Build a verse
kr.play("kick", kr.hit() * 4)
kr.play("bass", kr.seq("A2", "D3", None, "E2").over(2))
kr.save("verse")

# Build a chorus
kr.play("kick", kr.hit() * 8)
kr.play("bass", kr.seq("A3", "C3", "E3", "G3"))
kr.save("chorus")

# Switch between them
kr.recall("verse")
kr.recall("chorus")

docs.title: File persistence
docs.text:
Export the full session to a Python file:

kr.export("verse.py")

Reload it in a later session:

kr.load("verse.py")

Load from a subdirectory:

kr.load("songs/verse.py")

docs.title: What gets captured
docs.text:
A scene snapshot (both in-memory and file export) captures:

  • Voices -- all active voices with their DSP type and gain
  • Buses -- all effect buses and their configuration
  • Sends -- all send routings and levels
  • Patterns -- all active pattern assignments
  • Tempo -- current BPM
  • Master gain -- kr.master level
  • Controls -- current values of all voice/bus controls

docs.title: Music as Python modules
docs.text:
Exported files are plain Python. You can edit them, share them, and version them with git. A typical exported file sets up voices, buses, sends, and patterns -- everything needed to reproduce the session.

Write reusable setup scripts by hand:

# my_kit.py -- reusable drum kit setup
@kr.dsp
def kick() -> krs.Signal:
    gate = krs.control("gate", 0.0, 0.0, 1.0)
    env = krs.adsr(0.001, 0.25, 0.0, 0.05, gate)
    return krs.sine_osc(55.0 + env * 200.0) * env * 0.9

@kr.dsp
def hat() -> krs.Signal:
    gate = krs.control("gate", 0.0, 0.0, 1.0)
    env = krs.adsr(0.001, 0.04, 0.0, 0.02, gate)
    return krs.highpass(krs.white_noise(), 8000.0) * env * 0.5

with kr.batch():
    kr.voice("drums/kick", kick, gain=0.8)
    kr.voice("drums/hat", hat, gain=0.5)

Then load it from the REPL:

kr.load("my_kit.py")
kr.play("drums/kick", kr.hit() * 4)
kr.play("drums/hat", (kr.rest() + kr.hit()) * 4)

docs.title: Workflow: compose, export, iterate
docs.text:
1. Compose -- build your session interactively in the REPL
  2. Export -- kr.export("session.py") saves everything
  3. Iterate -- edit the file, reload with kr.load(), repeat
# Session 1: build the initial arrangement
kr.tempo = 128
kr.voice("kick", kick, gain=0.8)
kr.voice("bass", acid_bass, gain=0.3)
kr.play("kick", kr.hit() * 4)
kr.play("bass", kr.seq("A2", "D3", None, "E2").over(2))
kr.export("track_v1.py")

# Session 2: pick up where you left off
kr.load("track_v1.py")
kr.voice("lead", lead_fn, gain=0.25)
kr.play("lead", kr.seq("A4", "C5", "E5", "D5").over(2))
kr.export("track_v2.py")

docs.title: Swing and Warps
docs.text: Swing delays every other subdivision to create a shuffled, off-grid feel. krach implements swing as a piecewise linear time warp -- a general mechanism that can express any groove transformation.

docs.title: .swing(amount, grid)
docs.text:
Apply swing to any pattern:

pattern.swing(amount)
pattern.swing(amount, grid)

amount -- how much to delay the offbeat:

Value Feel 0.5 Straight (no swing) 0.67 Standard swing (triplet feel) 0.75 Heavy triplet swing

grid -- subdivisions per cycle. Defaults to 8 (8th notes in 4/4).

(kr.hit() * 8).swing(0.67)       # swung 8th notes
(kr.hit() * 8).swing(0.67, 8)    # same, explicit grid
(kr.hit() * 16).swing(0.6, 16)   # swung 16th notes

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
Swing operates on pairs of beats within the grid. For each pair, the second beat is shifted forward in time by the swing amount:

  • At amount=0.5, the second beat sits exactly halfway -- straight time
  • At amount=0.67, the second beat sits 2/3 of the way through the pair -- classic triplet swing
  • At amount=0.75, the second beat sits 3/4 of the way -- heavy shuffle

The time warp is piecewise linear within each beat pair. This means all events between the pair boundaries are smoothly redistributed, not just the beats themselves.

docs.title: Swung hi-hat
docs.text:
@kr.dsp
def hat() -> krs.Signal:
    gate = krs.control("gate", 0.0, 0.0, 1.0)
    env = krs.adsr(0.001, 0.04, 0.0, 0.02, gate)
    return krs.highpass(krs.white_noise(), 8000.0) * env * 0.5

kr.voice("hat", hat, gain=0.5)

# Straight 8ths
kr.play("hat", kr.hit() * 8)

# Standard swing
kr.play("hat", (kr.hit() * 8).swing(0.67))

# Heavy shuffle
kr.play("hat", (kr.hit() * 8).swing(0.75))

docs.title: Shuffled bass line
docs.text: kr.play("bass", kr.seq("A2", "D3", None, "E2").over(2).swing(0.67))

docs.title: Swung kick pattern
docs.text: kr.play("kick", (kr.hit() * 8).swing(0.67))

docs.title: Convenience kwarg on kr.play()
docs.text:
Instead of calling .swing() on the pattern, pass swing= directly to kr.play():

kr.play("kick", kr.hit() * 8, swing=0.67)
kr.play("hat", kr.hit() * 8, swing=0.67)

Both forms are equivalent.

docs.title: Combining swing with other transforms
docs.text:
Swing composes with all pattern operations:

# Swung euclidean rhythm
kr.play("hat", kr.hit().spread(5, 8).swing(0.67))

# Swing with periodic reverse
p = (kr.hit() * 8).swing(0.67)
kr.play("kick", p.every(4, lambda p: p.reverse()))

# Swung mini-notation
kr.play("hat", kr.p("x . x x . x . x").swing(0.67))

docs.title: Warp as a general time transform
docs.text:
Swing is one instance of a general time warp -- a function that remaps event positions within a cycle. The same mechanism can express:

  • Humanize -- small random offsets per event
  • Groove templates -- MPC-style per-step timing
  • Accelerando/ritardando -- gradual tempo curves within a cycle

Currently, .swing() is the built-in warp. The underlying warp system is designed to support additional transforms in the future.

docs.title: Synth Design
docs.text: krach lets you define synthesizers as plain Python functions. The @kr.dsp decorator transpiles your code to FAUST, which compiles to native audio via LLVM JIT. You write Python, you hear audio -- no manual compilation step.

docs.title: The @kr.dsp decorator
docs.text:
The pipeline: Python function --> FAUST .dsp --> LLVM JIT --> audio node.

@kr.dsp
def acid_bass() -> krs.Signal:
    freq = krs.control("freq", 55.0, 20.0, 800.0)
    gate = krs.control("gate", 0.0, 0.0, 1.0)
    cutoff = krs.control("cutoff", 800.0, 100.0, 4000.0)
    env = krs.adsr(0.005, 0.15, 0.3, 0.08, gate)
    return krs.lowpass(krs.saw(freq), cutoff) * env * 0.55

After decoration, acid_bass is a DspDef object. Pass it directly to kr.voice():

kr.voice("bass", acid_bass, gain=0.3)

The decorator saves both the Python source (.py) and the generated FAUST code (.dsp) to ~/.krach/dsp/.

docs.title: krs primitives reference
docs.text: All DSP building blocks live in the krs namespace (krach.dsp).

docs.title: Oscillators
docs.text: Function Description krs.sine_osc(freq) Sine oscillator krs.saw(freq) Sawtooth oscillator krs.square(freq) Square wave oscillator krs.phasor(freq) 0--1 ramp at freq Hz krs.white_noise() White noise generator

docs.title: Filters
docs.text: Function Description krs.lowpass(sig, cutoff) Butterworth lowpass -- signal first, cutoff Hz second krs.highpass(sig, cutoff) Butterworth highpass -- signal first, cutoff Hz second krs.bandpass(sig, cutoff, q) Bandpass filter -- signal first

docs.title: Envelopes
docs.text: Function Description krs.adsr(a, d, s, r, gate) ADSR envelope generator

docs.title: Effects
docs.text: Function Description krs.reverb(sig, room) Freeverb -- signal first, room size 0.0--1.0

docs.title: Controls
docs.text: Function Description krs.control(name, init, lo, hi) Exposed parameter (automatable from patterns)

docs.title: Controls: krs.control(name, init, lo, hi)
docs.text:
Controls are the bridge between patterns and audio. Each control becomes an automatable parameter on the voice.

freq = krs.control("freq", 55.0, 20.0, 800.0)
#                   name   init   lo     hi
  • name -- label used in pattern binding (bass/freq)
  • init -- default value
  • lo / hi -- valid range (used by MIDI mapping and modulation)

Two controls are conventional for melodic synths:

  • "freq" -- pitch in Hz (set by kr.note())
  • "gate" -- trigger (set by kr.note() and kr.hit())

You can add any number of custom controls:

cutoff = krs.control("cutoff", 800.0, 100.0, 4000.0)
resonance = krs.control("res", 0.5, 0.0, 1.0)
drive = krs.control("drive", 0.0, 0.0, 1.0)

docs.title: Signal arithmetic
docs.text:
krs.Signal supports standard arithmetic. Use *, +, and - to combine signals and scalars:

# Scale an oscillator by an envelope
krs.saw(freq) * env

# Mix two oscillators
krs.saw(freq) + krs.square(freq) * 0.3

# Offset a control (filter envelope modulation)
cutoff + filt_env * 1200.0

# Invert a signal
-env + 1.0

These operations translate directly to FAUST arithmetic in the generated code.

docs.title: Multi-input effects (buses)
docs.text:
Effects that receive audio from other voices -- reverb, delay, compressors -- must be defined with audio inputs. Create them with kr.bus(), not kr.voice():

@kr.dsp
def simple_reverb() -> krs.Signal:
    room = krs.control("room", 0.6, 0.0, 1.0)
    sig = krs.control("in0", 0.0, -1.0, 1.0)  # audio input
    return krs.reverb(sig, room)

kr.bus("verb", simple_reverb, gain=0.3)
kr.send("bass", "verb", level=0.4)

See Effect Routing for the full send/wire system.

docs.title: Hot reload
docs.text:
Edit your .py synth file, and the system picks up changes automatically:

  1. The @kr.dsp decorator re-transpiles Python to FAUST
  2. The FAUST file watcher detects the updated .dsp
  3. LLVM recompiles the DSP to native code
  4. The audio graph hot-swaps the node with a crossfade

No restart needed. Just redefine the function and call kr.voice() again:

# Change the synth -- hear the difference immediately
@kr.dsp
def acid_bass() -> krs.Signal:
    freq = krs.control("freq", 55.0, 20.0, 800.0)
    gate = krs.control("gate", 0.0, 0.0, 1.0)
    cutoff = krs.control("cutoff", 800.0, 100.0, 4000.0)
    env = krs.adsr(0.005, 0.15, 0.3, 0.08, gate)
    filt_env = krs.adsr(0.005, 0.2, 0.2, 0.1, gate)
    # Added filter envelope modulation
    return krs.lowpass(krs.saw(freq), cutoff + filt_env * 1200.0) * env * 0.55

kr.voice("bass", acid_bass, gain=0.3)  # hot-swaps, patterns keep playing

docs.title: Acid bass
docs.text:
A classic 303-style bass with filter envelope:

@kr.dsp
def acid_bass() -> krs.Signal:
    freq = krs.control("freq", 55.0, 20.0, 800.0)
    gate = krs.control("gate", 0.0, 0.0, 1.0)
    cutoff = krs.control("cutoff", 800.0, 100.0, 4000.0)
    env = krs.adsr(0.005, 0.15, 0.3, 0.08, gate)
    filt_env = krs.adsr(0.005, 0.2, 0.2, 0.1, gate)
    return krs.lowpass(krs.saw(freq), cutoff + filt_env * 1200.0) * env * 0.55

docs.title: Kick drum
docs.text:
Sine wave with pitch envelope for the characteristic thump:

@kr.dsp
def kick() -> krs.Signal:
    gate = krs.control("gate", 0.0, 0.0, 1.0)
    env = krs.adsr(0.001, 0.25, 0.0, 0.05, gate)
    return krs.sine_osc(55.0 + env * 200.0) * env * 0.9

docs.title: Hi-hat
docs.text:
Filtered white noise with a sharp envelope:

@kr.dsp
def hat() -> krs.Signal:
    gate = krs.control("gate", 0.0, 0.0, 1.0)
    env = krs.adsr(0.001, 0.04, 0.0, 0.02, gate)
    return krs.highpass(krs.white_noise(), 8000.0) * env * 0.5

docs.title: Pad
docs.text:
A detuned saw pair with slow attack for ambient textures:

@kr.dsp
def pad() -> krs.Signal:
    freq = krs.control("freq", 220.0, 20.0, 2000.0)
    gate = krs.control("gate", 0.0, 0.0, 1.0)
    cutoff = krs.control("cutoff", 2000.0, 200.0, 8000.0)
    env = krs.adsr(0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.8, gate)
    osc = krs.saw(freq) + krs.saw(freq * 1.005) * 0.8
    return krs.lowpass(osc, cutoff) * env * 0.3

docs.title: Simple reverb
docs.text:
A bus effect using Freeverb:

@kr.dsp
def simple_reverb() -> krs.Signal:
    room = krs.control("room", 0.6, 0.0, 1.0)
    sig = krs.control("in0", 0.0, -1.0, 1.0)
    return krs.reverb(sig, room)

docs.title: Putting it together
docs.text:
@kr.dsp
def kick() -> krs.Signal:
    gate = krs.control("gate", 0.0, 0.0, 1.0)
    env = krs.adsr(0.001, 0.25, 0.0, 0.05, gate)
    return krs.sine_osc(55.0 + env * 200.0) * env * 0.9

@kr.dsp
def acid_bass() -> krs.Signal:
    freq = krs.control("freq", 55.0, 20.0, 800.0)
    gate = krs.control("gate", 0.0, 0.0, 1.0)
    cutoff = krs.control("cutoff", 800.0, 100.0, 4000.0)
    env = krs.adsr(0.005, 0.15, 0.3, 0.08, gate)
    return krs.lowpass(krs.saw(freq), cutoff) * env * 0.55

with kr.batch():
    kr.voice("kick", kick, gain=0.8)
    kr.voice("bass", acid_bass, gain=0.3)

kr.tempo = 128
kr.play("kick", kr.hit() * 4)
kr.play("bass", kr.seq("A2", "D3", None, "E2").over(2))
kr.play("bass/cutoff", kr.mod_sine(200, 2000).over(4))
